segments.title: Why Guadeloupe mattered in February 1810
segments.summary: The opening places the invasion's truce and surrender in early February 1810 and connects the campaign to British amphibious forces and colonial marines.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke dates the fighting from 28 January to 6 February, with a truce after the night battle of 3-4 February and surrender following on 5 February. He uses the anniversary to frame the operation as a useful historical case for thinking about marines, locally raised forces, and expeditionary warfare. The discussion also flags uncertainty over exactly which colonial-marine contingent took part.

evidence.note: The transcript dates the campaign, truce, and surrender and introduces its relevance to marines and colonial forces.

segments.title: Builder's Old Measurement and sailing-ship tonnage
segments.summary: A technical aside explains the BM tonnage notation used for sailing ships and why its cargo-capacity formula does not translate cleanly to powered vessels.
segments.body: The video explains Builder's Old Measurement, often printed as tons burthen or tons BM, as a historical estimate based on hull dimensions and carrying capacity. Dr. Clarke walks through the formula and cautions that engines, fuel, and other machinery make it unsuitable for comparing modern ships. This is a useful decoding note for readers encountering eighteenth- and nineteenth-century ship specifications.

evidence.note: The transcript defines BM, gives its dimensional formula, and explains its use as an Age of Sail carrying-capacity measure.

segments.title: Caribbean wealth and Britain's peripheral war strategy
segments.summary: The strategic background links valuable Caribbean colonies to Britain's method of financing continental allies while attacking enemy possessions around the world's periphery.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke contrasts the commercial value and administrative importance of Caribbean possessions with the former Thirteen Colonies. With France and Spain heavily committed in Europe, Britain could use naval mobility and relatively small expeditionary forces to seize overseas territory and redirect trade. The campaign therefore appears as part of a wider maritime strategy, not an isolated island battle.

evidence.note: The transcript compares the value of Caribbean and Canadian possessions with the Thirteen Colonies.
Britain's war method is summarized as attacking the periphery while financing allied land warfare in Europe.

segments.title: The West Indies campaign's road to Guadeloupe
segments.summary: A regional survey traces British operations through the Danish West Indies, failed French relief expeditions, Martinique, and finally Guadeloupe.
segments.body: The invasion closes a longer West Indies campaign running through the Napoleonic Wars. Dr. Clarke notes Alexander Cochrane's bloodless seizure of the Danish islands in 1807, British interception of French relief forces, and the invasion of Martinique before Guadeloupe became the last major French position. The sequence shows the Royal Navy progressively stripping France and its allies of Caribbean bases.

evidence.note: The transcript surveys the 1803-1814 West Indies campaign and names the Danish islands, relief expeditions, Martinique, and Guadeloupe.

segments.title: Cochrane's self-supporting Caribbean war machine
segments.summary: Cochrane combines naval command, colonial revenue, captured French vessels, West India regiments, and colonial marines into a locally sustained expeditionary force.
segments.body: The video presents Alexander Cochrane as an unusually resourceful theater commander who could raise troops, recommission captured ships, and draw on wealthy colonial governments. Captured French vessels provided material and operational capacity, while West India regiments and colonial marines supplied locally recruited manpower, including formerly enslaved men. These arrangements made the campaign substantially self-supporting and built experience later used in the War of 1812.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Cochrane's authority, colonial funding, captured ships, and ability to sustain his squadron.
West India regiments and colonial marines recruited from formerly enslaved people are identified as part of the force.

segments.title: HMS Diamond Rock, the Royal Navy's stone sloop
segments.summary: The Diamond Rock episode shows the Royal Navy commissioning a fortified island as a sloop and forcing a major French fleet to reduce it before Trafalgar.
segments.body: When the army declined to garrison Diamond Rock, the Royal Navy commissioned the position as HMS Diamond Rock and defended it with sailors and heavy guns. Villeneuve's fleet eventually overwhelmed the hundred-man garrison during its Caribbean movement before Trafalgar, but only after the rock had held out for months. The detailed armament and difficult approaches explain why a fixed rock could function as a small warship and sea-control outpost.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Hood's capture, naval commissioning, the hundred-man garrison, and Villeneuve's eventual attack.
The transcript describes the rock's 18-pounders, 24-pounder, carronades, and defended landing approaches.

segments.title: Cochrane and Beckwith's aggressive command team
segments.summary: Profiles of Admiral Alexander Cochrane and Lieutenant-General George Beckwith explain the experienced, forward-leading command culture behind the invasion.
segments.body: Cochrane commanded the Leeward Islands station with extensive regional authority, while Beckwith refused to remain behind as a coordinating governor and instead accompanied the expedition. Accounts place Beckwith at the front, directing troops around a French mine, while Cochrane maneuvered and fired from his ships. Their shared aggressiveness mattered because it was coupled to experience and a clear division of responsibilities rather than independent action.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces Cochrane's station command and later North American and Jamaican responsibilities.
Beckwith's determination to join the action and his reported front-line direction around a mine are described.

segments.title: Joint planning without radios at Guadeloupe
segments.summary: The invasion demonstrates how experienced army and navy officers coordinated a fast amphibious campaign through shared planning rather than real-time communications.
segments.body: Cochrane and Beckwith could not coordinate by radio, so their effectiveness depended on prior discussion, mutual understanding, and subordinate commanders accustomed to working together. The force assembled and executed the campaign within weeks after earlier operations such as Martinique. Dr. Clarke compares that tempo to the Falklands campaign and argues that the resulting synchronization was exceptional even by later standards.

evidence.note: The transcript describes prior joint planning, experienced subordinates, the weeks-long assembly period, and earlier shared operations.

segments.title: Bypassing French forts while the fleet suppresses them
segments.summary: British troops avoid the fortified main road and move toward their objective while overwhelming naval fire contains the French defenses.
segments.body: The French concentrated resources in a few strong positions and expected the invaders to approach along the obvious route. British troops instead used an alternate road, leaving the fixed defenses to face far greater naval firepower while the army advanced on the city. The episode illustrates a core amphibious advantage: sea power can fix or suppress a coastal strongpoint without making its capture the army's immediate objective.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts French investment in the main-road fort with the British bypass and heavy naval bombardment.

segments.title: Tropical health as an operational advantage
segments.summary: British attention to hygiene, food, sleep, manpower, and stagnant water preserved a healthier invasion force than the disease-stricken defenders.
segments.body: Disease was a major threat on the Caribbean station, but the British force entered the operation comparatively healthy and worked to keep it that way. Dr. Clarke highlights fresh food and vegetables, adequate rest, sufficient troop numbers, and removal of stagnant water as practical measures. Health discipline therefore becomes part of combat power: it preserved the force available for maneuver while illness weakened the French position.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts French disease problems with British hygiene, nutrition, rest, force size, and stagnant-water controls.
